summary: Jeff Atwood highlights a 2003 column where tech journalist John Dvorak complained that Windows' System Idle Process was 'hogging' 95% of his CPU resources, demonstrating a fundamental misunderstanding of how computers work. Atwood explains that the idle process is simply a placeholder task that runs when the CPU has nothing else to do, and a high idle percentage actually means the system is working fine. The post uses this anecdote to gently mock a prominent tech writer for not understanding basic computer science concepts despite decades in the industry. Atwood provides the Wikipedia explanation of idle tasks to educate readers on why this 'problem' is actually normal system behavior.
key_points: John Dvorak, a veteran tech journalist, publicly complained about the System Idle Process consuming CPU cycles, not understanding it represents unused capacity
The idle task is a fundamental concept in operating systems that runs only when there's nothing else for the computer to do
On modern processors, the idle task executes HLT instructions that save power and reduce heat
A high System Idle Process percentage is normal and desirable—it means your CPU isn't overworked
Dvorak likely had an unrelated system problem that he incorrectly blamed on the idle process
money_quotes: From the 'you can't make this stuff up department'
When I hit Ctrl-Alt-Delete, I see that the System Idle Process is hogging all the resources and chewing up 95 percent of the processor's cycles. Doing what? Doing nothing?
His profound misunderstanding of how this fundamental bit of computer science works is a wee bit disturbing for a computer journalist of his tenure and stature
If the idle task is 'chewing up 95 percent of the processor's cycles,' that's normal: it simply means your CPU isn't working very hard on anything at the moment
key_insight: Even veteran technology journalists can have embarrassing gaps in their understanding of fundamental computing concepts, which should remind us all to verify our assumptions before publishing opinions.
